text: Here we can see the panoramic view once we enter the thorax including the spine, ozygous vein, the right lung which falls anteriorly exposing the esophagus.
We turn on the firefly mode of the system which shows the thoracic duct nicely illuminated because the orogreen dye has gone from the inguinal nodes into the duct.
We place our ports and then start with the dissection.
The first step is to divide the inferior pulmonary ligament on the right side.
Once this is done, we take the anterior pleural cut in the infra-oxygous part.
This allows us to separate the esophagus from the pericardium which lies anterior to it.
We take care to include all the fibrophytic tissue and lymph nodes along with our specimen.
With gentle blunt and sharp dissection we can separate out the esophagus from the overlying pericardium.

Here we come towards the right main bronchus and here we can see the subcarinal lymph nodes and also the 109R that is right main bronchus lymph node which in this patient were conglomerated and stuck together.
We do a little bit of the dissection and then turn our attention to the posterior pleural cut.
This cut is made under firefly guidance taking care to stay away from the thoracic duct and not cause any injury to this duct.
The firefly mode and the ICG really helps us to delineate the course of the thoracic duct.
Here we can see the left sided pleura coming into the picture.
We then proceed cordially and take the pleural cut right up to the diaphragm.
The pleural cut is completed.
Here we can see the station 1-1-1 that is the supradiaphragmatic lymph nodes which are taken along with the specimen.
The pleura is dissected away.
we take care to include all the fibrophytic tissue as this is the component of total mesoesophageal resection.
We can see the paraesophageal that is 110 lymph nodes coming along with the specimen.
Under the Firefly guidance, we can take care not to cause any injury to the thoracic duct and include all the fibrophytic tissue in our resection.
On the left posterior lateral aspect, we run into the direct aortic branches from the descending thoracic aorta and going directly to the esophagus.

We can now sling the esophagus with the help of a piece of umbilical tape and the assistant can provide counter traction which helps to go behind the esophagus on the posterior lateral side and separate out the mesentery of the esophagus.

we come across the area of the growth and we found that there is some adherence with the thoracic duct and the fibrophytic tissues on this side are fused probably due to the post radiation sequelae and the bulky growth we try to separate out but because the thoracic duct was stuck to the growth we took the decision to clip

and ligate the thoracic duct in continuity to avoid any chances of chile in post-operative period we then turned our attention to the ventral aspect of the dissection and dissected out the station 107 that is the subcarinal node in most of the patients these nodes are anthracotic and they are stuck to the underlying airway
we were able to separate out these nodes and then turned our attention back towards the left posterior lateral aspect.
And here we can see the mesoesophageal tissue which we are trying to capture along with the specimen in total.
We can see the station 109 that is the left main bronchus nodes and also the 107 that is the subcarinal nodes.

and both of these nodes are part of our lymphadenectomy template so we will be taking them we have to be careful with precise bursts of electrocautery as we are going very close to the carina and the major airway once the node is taken we can then go behind and cranially and dissect out the esophagus
This is the area of the tracheoesophageal groove where we will come across left recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The assistant can help at this stage with the suction and compress the membranous trachea very carefully while we are dissecting the esophagus away from it.
We can see the azagus vein and the bronchial artery coming into the picture.
We usually clip the azagus vein and try to preserve the bronchial artery.
But in this patient, this was not possible as the bronchial artery was also stuck to the tumor which was exactly in that area.
Once the azagus vein is ligated, we can take the fibrophytic tissue immediately behind the azagus vein.
We also coagulate the bronchial artery.

and sacrifice it then we can proceed with the suprazygous part of the dissection and we can see the left recurrent laryngeal group of lymph nodes coming into view this is the station 1 0 6 L that is the left left recurrent laryngeal lymph node

again in this area the electrocautery we use has to be very precise and small bursts of energy to avoid any electro thermal injury to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve which runs exactly below the nodes in this area nodes are taken out and the assistant can take these nodes out we then continue with mobilizing the esophagus going more and more cranially

Care has to be taken to coordinate our movements with the respiratory movements of the patient and not to put the cautery too close to the membranous trachea as it can lead to a delayed thermal injury.
The mobilization is almost complete and we can see the structures.
at this point we also go beyond the esophagus to the left main bronchus lymph nodes so the left main bronchus nodes are also taken as part of the template
The esophageal mobilization is now complete and we can see all the structures including the trachea, perina, left main bronchus and right main bronchus.
Once the complete mobilization is done, we place an intercostal drain.
It is our usual practice to give a wax-guided paravertebral block in 4-5 intercostal spaces as this reduces the postoperative pain from the port side.
